strategic_overview: The problem sits exactly at the gap between nonuniform relative computability and parameter-exact existential definability. The first crucial reduction is topological: computable AUT-countability on a cone implies Aut(A) is countable in the fixed computable copy, hence as a closed subgroup of S_infinity it is discrete and has a finite base b. Thus every automorphism is uniquely determined by the image of b. If one could show that the global action relation Act_b(q,x,y), meaning 'the unique automorphism sending b to q sends x to y', is Sigma^in_1-definable over one fixed finite tuple, then the requested example cannot exist. This is the most promising line, and it should be attacked using the intrinsic definability and existential-atomicity machinery from Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone, https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3842, Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/EAtomicStructures.pdf, and A Robuster Scott Rank, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/scottRank.pdf.

directions.name: Finite-base reduction and global action relation
directions.description: Start by proving the finite-base reduction rigorously. Since A is computable and all automorphisms of A are computable relative to A plus the cone base C, Aut(A) is countable. As a closed subgroup of S_infinity it is discrete, so there is a finite tuple b whose pointwise stabilizer is trivial. Define Act_b(q,x,y) in the expansion (A,b): q is in the orbit of b and y is the value at x of the unique automorphism taking b to q. If Act_b is Sigma^in_1-definable over b and one additional fixed finite tuple r, then taking a=b r refutes the desired example, because for every pi, Graph(pi)(x,y) is Act_b(pi(b),x,y), using only a union pi(a) as allowed parameters.
key_ideas: Reduce the problem from individual automorphism graphs to one global action relation indexed by finite images of a base.
Try to show that computable AUT-countability on a cone forces Act_b to be relatively intrinsically c.e. on a cone, or at least Sigma^in_1 after finitely many fixed parameters.

If this works, the answer to the original question is negative: choosing the finite base plus the fixed uniformizing parameters gives a finite parameter set over which every automorphism graph is Sigma^in_1-definable.
relevant_techniques: Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group, https://homepages.ecs.vuw.ac.nz/~melnikal/permutation%20groups.pdf: the introductory observation identifies Aut(A) of a computable structure as an effectively closed subgroup of S_infinity; Proposition 4.1 and the surrounding discrete-group analysis support the finite-base/discreteness reduction.

Computable Functors and Effective Interpretability, https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.02737: the quoted AKMS-Chisholm intrinsic definability theorem says uniform relative c.e. behavior gives computable infinitary Sigma^c_1 definitions; this is the exact bridge needed if Act_b can be made uniform.

Computability theoretic classifications for classes of structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/ClassesOfStructures.pdf: Theorem 3.10 gives r.i.c.e. iff computably infinitary Sigma^c_1 definability with finite parameters; the task is to force the parameters to be fixed once and for all.

Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/EAtomicStructures.pdf: Lemma 6.2 is the key finite-use/permutation extraction argument turning a uniform computable operator into existential support formulas.

Computable Polish Group Actions, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/balls.pdf: Lemma 16 and Theorem 17 give Baire-category and orbit-map tools for turning many pointwise computations into uniform information on a cone.
directions.potential_obstacles: The hypothesis is strongly nonuniform: for each copy and each automorphism there may be a different Turing index, and that index may hardwire finitely many helper elements of the copy. Existing intrinsic definability theorems often allow extra finite parameters depending on the relation; the original question forbids extra parameters beyond a union pi(a). The hard point is to prove that all those hidden helper parameters can be replaced by one fixed finite tuple r.

directions.name: Parameter-restricted relation-spectrum contrapositive
directions.description: Fix the finite base b and an automorphism pi. In the rigid expansion E_pi=(A,b,pi(b)), the graph of pi is an invariant binary relation. If it is not Sigma^in_1-definable without extra parameters in E_pi, try to build a copy of E_pi in which the transported graph is not computable from the copy plus the cone oracle. Such a copy would contradict computable AUT-countability. This would give a direct no-go theorem, but it requires a parameter-restricted strengthening of the usual relation-spectrum results.
key_ideas: Use b to make the graph of pi invariant: because b is a base, the expansion by b and pi(b) is rigid.
Adapt Harizanov/Ash-Nerode free-tuple coding so that no hidden finite parameters are allowed in the definition.
If non-Sigma^in_1 over b union pi(b) always yields a copy with noncomputable graph, then computable AUT-countability forces every pi to be definable over b union pi(b), refuting the desired example.
relevant_techniques: Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone, https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3842: Section 2.3 equates intrinsic Sigma^0_alpha on a cone with Sigma^in_alpha definability; Corollary 2.7 gives the c.e.-degree fullness dichotomy once a relation is not intrinsically computable on a cone.

Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone, https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.3842: Theorem 5.1 and Proposition 5.4 supply 2-free tuple methods for turning failure of low definability into copies with higher relation degree.

A relation on (omega, <) of intermediate degree spectrum on a cone, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.01071: Theorem 3.2 and the coding-sequence machinery show how to make parameter-sensitive finite-gadget coding robust on a cone.
A Robuster Scott Rank, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/scottRank.pdf: Lemma 3.2 gives the alpha=1 type-omitting tool for unsupported Pi^in_1 types.
directions.potential_obstacles: The standard relation-spectrum theorems allow finite parameters in the infinitary definition, exactly the loophole exploited by the problem. A graph may be computable in every copy because the computation hardwires a pi-specific helper tuple, while still not being definable from b union pi(b). A new parameter-restricted spectrum theorem is needed; it may be false without using rigidity or uniqueness of the automorphism.

directions.name: Private-key counterexample construction
directions.description: If the uniformization/no-go approach fails, try to construct A with a countable discrete automorphism group and a finite base b, but with automorphisms pi_n whose graphs require private finite key tuples k_n. The intended pattern is: Graph(pi_n) is Sigma^in_1-definable over b, k_n, pi_n(b), pi_n(k_n), and computable in every copy by hardwiring the image of k_n, but for every finite parameter set a there is some n whose key orbit is missed by a, and Graph(pi_n) is not Sigma^in_1-definable over a union pi_n(a).
key_ideas: Use a countable discrete group action, not independent local swaps; independent swaps have uncountable closure and cannot be the automorphism group of a countable structure with computably AUT-countability.
Build infinitely many key orbits, one for each automorphism or family of automorphisms, while keeping a finite base for the whole group.
Attach rigid gadgets whose relevant correspondence is existentially visible only when the matching key is named.
Make every automorphism individually computable in arbitrary copies by allowing the Turing machine for that automorphism to hardwire finitely many key images.
relevant_techniques: Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group, https://homepages.ecs.vuw.ac.nz/~melnikal/permutation%20groups.pdf: Lemma 4.3 realizes closed subgroups by orbit-relation structures; Lemma 4.4 and Proposition 2.3 give rigid-order blow-ups preserving automorphism groups while making structures computable.

Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/EAtomicStructures.pdf: Theorem 1.10 characterizes finite-parameter existential atomicity by Sigma^in_3 Scott sentences and computable categoricity on a cone; useful for designing gadgets that become simple only after naming a key.

On Optimal Scott Sentences of Finitely Generated Algebraic Structures, https://arxiv.org/abs/1702.06448, and Complexity of Scott Sentences, https://arxiv.org/abs/1807.02715: the finite-generation results warn that if a finite tuple term-generates the structure, automorphism graphs tend to become low-definable from the tuple and its image.

A Computable Functor from Graphs to Fields, https://arxiv.org/abs/1510.07322: Theorem 1.2 and Theorem 9.9 can transfer a graph-based construction to fields while preserving automorphism spectra, if a graph example is found.
directions.potential_obstacles: The closure constraint on automorphism groups is severe: a direct sum of countably many independent finite switches closes to an uncountable product. Regular or Cayley-style countable group actions often make every automorphism graph Sigma^in_1 from a base and its image via a disjunction over words. The construction must avoid term-connectedness while still allowing all automorphisms to be computable in arbitrary presentations.

directions.name: Type-omitting and copy-switching attack on unsupported graph types
directions.description: Develop a direct contrapositive: assume that for a finite base b and some pi, the graph type of pi over b union pi(b) is not existentially supported. Use a type-omitting construction inside a class of partial automorphism expansions to build a copy B of A and a conjugate automorphism pi^B whose graph is not computable from B plus the cone oracle. This would prove that computable AUT-countability forces existential support for all automorphism graphs.
key_ideas: View y=pi(x) as a relation between pointed tuple types in the expansion by b and pi(b).
If no Sigma^in_1 formula supports the corresponding Pi^in_1 information, omit or split that type in a new model/copy.
Diagonalize against all candidate B plus C computations of the automorphism by waiting for finite use and then switching to an existentially indistinguishable but graph-incompatible configuration.
relevant_techniques: A Robuster Scott Rank, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/scottRank.pdf: Lemma 3.2 omits unsupported Pi^in_alpha types in models of Pi^in_{alpha+1} sentences; the alpha=1 case is the relevant one.

Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/EAtomicStructures.pdf: Lemma 5.3 omits unsupported universal types in Pi_2 classes, and Section 4 turns existential support of universal types into orbit definitions.

Punctual Definability on Structures, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/coneFeb.pdf: Proposition 4.3 gives an omitted-orbit copy-switching diagonalization; Theorem 4.4 shows how infinite orbit choices defeat candidate procedures.

On the computability of optimal Scott sentences, https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.09626: the labelled bouquet/direct-limit constructions in Theorem 1.2 show how to keep finite stages isomorphic while separating the limits.
directions.potential_obstacles: One must define the right Pi_2 class of expansions so that the reduct remains a copy of A and the extra relation really is an automorphism graph. Type omission may produce a model satisfying a weak theory but not isomorphic to A unless a sufficiently strong Scott or back-and-forth condition is built in. This direction is technically demanding but targets the exact model-theoretic obstruction.

directions.name: Analytic equivalence relation and cone-uniformization route
directions.description: Code automorphisms, finite partial automorphisms, or pointed automorphism graphs as reals and study the relevant equivalence relation under conjugacy by copies. The goal is to show that if finite-parameter Sigma^in_1 definitions fail too often, then there is a perfect or high-rank family of automorphism patterns; the perfect-set coding methods should then produce a copy with an automorphism hyperarithmetic but not computable from the copy plus the cone oracle, contradicting computable AUT-countability.
key_ideas: Treat nondefinable automorphism graphs as an analytic family of inequivalent patterns.
If the family has a perfect set, code a noncomputable real into a graph or automorphism as in the Vaught-conjecture and analytic-equivalence-relation papers.
If there is no perfect set, use ranked equivalence relations with countable rank fibers to obtain cone uniformity.
relevant_techniques: A Computability Theoretic Equivalent to Vaught's Conjecture, https://arxiv.org/abs/1206.5682: Theorem 2.2 on scattered projective ranked equivalence relations, Section 5.1 perfect-set coding into back-and-forth types, and the cone-extraction techniques are directly relevant.

Analytic equivalence relations satisfying hyperarithmetic-is-recursive, https://arxiv.org/abs/1306.1513: Theorem 1.3, Lemma 2.1 Borel approximations, and Lemma 2.3 Barwise compactness plus overspill give the general hyperarithmetic-is-recursive on a cone template.

Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group, https://homepages.ecs.vuw.ac.nz/~melnikal/permutation%20groups.pdf: Proposition 4.1 gives hyperarithmetic orbit-equivalence for discrete effectively closed groups.
Computable Polish Group Actions, https://math.berkeley.edu/~antonio/papers/balls.pdf: Theorem 17 connects G_delta orbits and uniform computability on a cone via Effros's theorem.
directions.potential_obstacles: The object 'the graph of pi over a union pi(a)' is not invariant under arbitrary isomorphisms unless a contains a base or the stabilizer centralizes pi. Descriptive-set-theoretic cone theorems may yield hyperarithmetic or threshold-spectrum conclusions, but the original problem asks for exact Sigma^in_1 definability with a prescribed finite parameter set.

key_obstacles: The main barrier is nonuniformity. Computably AUT-countable on a cone says every individual automorphism of every copy is computable from that copy plus a fixed oracle, but the computing program may depend on the automorphism and on the copy and may hardwire finitely many unnamed elements. Standard AKMS-Chisholm intrinsic definability converts such behavior into Sigma^c_1 definitions only with extra finite parameters, while the question allows only a union pi(a). A second obstacle is invariance: the graph of a single automorphism is not an invariant relation in an unpointed structure; it becomes invariant only after naming a finite base and the image of that base. A third obstacle is the topology of Aut(A): countability forces discreteness and a finite base, ruling out easy independent-switch constructions because their closure is uncountable. Finally, many natural countable-group actions are too term-connected; once a finite base and its image are named, the automorphism graph is often a countable disjunction over words or terms and hence Sigma^in_1.
recommended_starting_point: Start with the finite-base/global-action route. Prove explicitly that Aut(A) is countable and hence has a finite base b. Then define Act_b(q,x,y) and try to prove the key lemma: if every section q |-> Act_b(q,.,.) is computable in every copy relative to the cone oracle, then Act_b is Sigma^in_1-definable over b plus one fixed finite tuple r. The first concrete step is to attempt a contraposition using the finite-use argument of Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures, Lemma 6.2, and the type-omitting lemma of A Robuster Scott Rank, Lemma 3.2. If the lemma is true, the original question has a negative answer by taking a=b r. If it fails, the failure should exhibit exactly the private-key mechanism needed for a counterexample construction.
